items.q: What did the Storming of the Bastille achieve militarily in 1789?
items.correct: Little militarily — the fortress held only seven prisoners — but it was a hugely powerful symbolic blow against royal authority.
items.misconception: The Bastille was a vast prison freed of thousands of political prisoners.
items.random_wrong: The Bastille was Louis XVI's main palace.
items.why: Students assume the Bastille was crammed with political prisoners. Its importance was symbolic, representing arbitrary royal power, not the scale of the liberation.

items.q: Who held power during the Terror of 1793-94?
items.correct: The Committee of Public Safety, dominated by Robespierre, directed the Terror, not the king, who had already been executed in January 1793.
items.misconception: Louis XVI ordered the executions of the Terror.
items.random_wrong: Napoleon ran the Terror before becoming emperor.
items.why: Students misplace the king's role; Louis XVI was guillotined before the Terror's height, which was driven by the radical Jacobin government.

items.q: Was Napoleon's 1799 rise a continuation of the revolution?
items.correct: Napoleon ended the revolutionary republic by seizing power in a coup, though he preserved some revolutionary reforms like legal equality.
items.misconception: Napoleon was elected by the French people to lead the republic.
items.random_wrong: Napoleon restored the Bourbon monarchy in 1799.
items.why: Learners overstate popular consent; Brumaire was a military coup, even if later plebiscites gave it a veneer of legitimacy.

items.q: What body did Louis XVI summon in 1789 to solve the financial crisis?
items.why: It's The Estates-General. “The National Assembly” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What event of 14 July 1789 symbolised the popular revolution?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The Storming of the Bastille. “The Tennis Court Oath” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Who dominated the Committee of Public Safety during the Terror?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Robespierre. “Danton” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How did Napoleon Bonaparte come to power in 1799?
items.correct: By a coup overthrowing the Directory
items.why: The answer examiners want is: By a coup overthrowing the Directory. “By winning a national election” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was the Bastille stormed?
items.why: Exact answer: 1789 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
